title: Fubo hikes prices by $15 after restoring some NBCU channels lost in November
gist: Fubo is raising all subscription prices by $15/month after striking a new carriage deal with NBCUniversal, but the restored lineup excludes nine channels NBCU spun off into Versant, leaving subscribers paying more than pre-blackout prices for less content than before.

insights.claim: Fubo is raising all of its subscription plan prices by $15 per month as it restores some NBCUniversal channels that had been dropped since November 2025, pushing prices above their pre-blackout levels.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: A vMVPD reversing a rare price cut back to (and past) pre-dispute levels signals limited pricing power against content owners despite subscriber pushback risk.
insights.quote: Fubo prices are going up by $15 per month because it will have some NBCUniversal channels again.

insights.claim: When Fubo lost NBCUniversal channels in the November 2025 blackout, it cut its Essential plan from $85 to $74/month, Pro from $85 to $75/month, and Elite from $95 to $84/month; the new $15 increase now puts Essential and Pro above their original $85 price and Elite above its original $95 price.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Investors/operators tracking vMVPD unit economics should note that carriage disputes with major programmers can end with subscribers paying a net premium versus the pre-dispute baseline, not a restored status quo.
insights.quote: The Essential plan went from $85 per month to $74 per month.

insights.claim: Despite the new deal and price hike, Fubo still cannot offer nine cable channels (CNBC, SYFY, USA Network, E!, and MS NOW, formerly MSNBC) because NBCUniversal spun those channels off into a separate company, Versant, in January 2026.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Corporate spinoffs of cable assets (Versant) are fragmenting carriage negotiations, meaning distributors like Fubo may need multiple separate deals to reassemble a lineup they once got in one contract.
insights.quote: Fubo subscribers still can’t watch nine cable channels that NBCUniversal spun off into the company Versant in January.

insights.claim: During the dispute, Fubo publicly accused NBCUniversal of trying to force it into a multi-year deal for Versant-bound channels that would extend well past the point those channels would be owned by the separate Versant company, calling it an attempt to make Fubo subscribers subsidize channels not worth their cost.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Content owners appear to be using multi-year contract terms to lock in economics across corporate restructurings (spinoffs), a tactic distributors should watch for and price into future carriage negotiations.
insights.quote: NBCU wants Fubo to sign a multi-year deal—well past the time the Versant channels will be owned by a separate company. NBCU wants Fubo subscribers to subsidize these channels.

insights.claim: Fubo had also sought to integrate NBCUniversal's Peacock streaming service into its channel store as part of the dispute, an ask that does not appear to have been resolved in the final settlement described in the article.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Watch for whether Fubo later announces Peacock aggregation, which would be a distinct commercial event beyond this carriage renewal.

insights.claim: The author infers it is plausible Fubo was forced to accept a higher per-channel cost in the new NBCUniversal deal than it paid before the blackout, which would explain why restoring even a partial lineup required a $15 price hike.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: If programmer-side pricing power is rising post-spinoff, other vMVPDs renewing NBCUniversal/Versant carriage deals could face similar cost pressure and pass-through price hikes.
insights.quote: It’s plausible that Fubo was forced to pay more for the channels than it previously did, which limited its options.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'But charging more for fewer channels'

insights.claim: The author argues that charging subscribers more for a smaller channel lineup than before the blackout is especially risky for Fubo because it is already struggling to compete with YouTube TV.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Fubo's relative competitive weakness versus YouTube TV makes it more exposed to subscriber attrition from this price increase than a market leader would be.
insights.quote: charging more for fewer channels is questionable, especially as Fubo is already challenged to compete with YouTube TV

insights.claim: The author had earlier praised Fubo's post-blackout price cut as a fair, slimmer-and-cheaper repositioning versus forced channel bundling, but now argues the reversal to higher prices for a partial lineup undercuts that value proposition and risks reminding subscribers why they left traditional cable.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: A vMVPD's core pitch versus cable is lower cost and lighter bundles; reverting to forced bundled price increases erodes the core differentiator that justified switching from cable in the first place.
insights.quote: By forcing customers to pay more for bundled channels they may not want, however, the sports streaming service is reminding people why they left cable in the first place.
